title: Pre-Trained Language Models for Interactive Decision-Making

structured.summary: This paper proposes LID, a framework leveraging pre-trained language models as sequential decision-making policies by encoding inputs as sequences and fine-tuning on task data. It demonstrates improved combinatorial generalization and data efficiency on simulated embodied environments, introducing an active data gathering loop to reduce reliance on expert demonstrations.

strengths.point: Effective use of pre-trained LMs to improve combinatorial generalization in sequential decision tasks
strengths.evidence: LID improves success rates by 43.6% on novel tasks in VirtualHome and outperforms baselines on BabyAI, showing strong generalization to unseen goal combinations.

strengths.point: Active Data Gathering (ADG) enables policy learning without expert demonstrations
strengths.evidence: LID-ADG significantly outperforms PPO, DQN+HER, and heuristic baselines on both in-distribution and novel tasks, bootstrapping further offline RL methods.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to recent LM-based interactive or few-shot policy learning methods
weaknesses.evidence: No empirical or conceptual baselines against recent state-of-the-art methods leveraging prompting or few-shot learning for embodied tasks are included.

weaknesses.point: Limited detail and justification in methodology choices and reproducibility
weaknesses.evidence: No explanation for GPT-2 selection over newer models, missing hyperparameters for active data gathering, and no code or pretrained model release statement.

questions.question: How would the approach perform with more recent or multimodal LMs instead of GPT-2?
questions.motivation: Determining if gains are model-dependent or general to Transformer architectures affects applicability and future improvements.

questions.question: Can the method scale to real-world or raw sensory input settings without hand-crafted relabeling?
questions.motivation: Assessing scalability and practicality in complex environments is crucial for broader impact beyond simulated tasks.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to recent LM-based interactive or few-shot policy learning methods. No empirical or conceptual baselines against recent state-of-the-art methods leveraging prompting or few-shot learning for embodied tasks are included
critique_points.point_002: Limited detail and justification in methodology choices and reproducibility. No explanation for GPT-2 selection over newer models, missing hyperparameters for active data gathering, and no code or pretrained model release statement
